title: Laws of Motion and Friction
subject: Physics
content:
# Laws of Motion and Friction

## Quick Synopsis

Newton’s laws convert forces into acceleration. Most JEE problems are won by drawing correct free-body diagrams and choosing axes that reduce components.

## Must Know

- First law defines inertia; second law gives net external force as rate of change of momentum.
- Third-law pairs act on different bodies and never cancel on the same free-body diagram.
- Normal reaction adjusts according to constraints; it is not always equal to mg.
- Static friction is self-adjusting up to limiting value; kinetic friction has nearly fixed magnitude μkN.
- Pseudo force appears in non-inertial frames and is opposite to frame acceleration.
- Circular motion needs net inward force, not a separate mysterious centripetal force.

## Formulas / Rules

- Fnet = ma for constant mass.
- Static friction: fs ≤ μsN; limiting friction: flim = μsN.
- Kinetic friction: fk = μkN.
- Banking without friction: tanθ = v²/rg.
- Pulley-string constraint: same string has same tension in ideal cases and related accelerations.

## JEE Focus

- Draw one FBD per body, not one diagram for the full system unless using system equations.
- For wedges and lifts, decide whether ground frame or accelerated frame is cleaner.
- Check impending motion direction before assigning static friction direction.
- In connected bodies, write constraint equations before solving force equations.

## Common Traps

- Friction can act in the direction of motion if it opposes relative slipping.
- Action-reaction forces never act on the same object.
- Tension can differ across a massive pulley or non-ideal string.
- Maximum static friction is not always acting.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you draw a correct FBD for blocks, pulleys, inclines, and wedges?
- Can you determine friction direction?
- Can you solve lift and pseudo-force questions?
- Can you use circular motion force balance?
